business_info: Each molecule in the database is assigned a tier code as part of the multi-phase toxicological assessment process.

Regulatory reviewers sometimes refer informally to “hotlist compounds,” which are molecules expected to move between tier codes over time as new toxicology data emerge; however, this label is not stored anywhere in the database schema.

In dossiers prepared for high-profile risk assessments, molecules may be flagged in narrative form as “sentinel compounds” when they are used primarily as indicators of broader exposure to a category of substances; this designation does not correspond directly to any tier_code value.

For legacy reasons, internal briefing decks sometimes group molecules into “priority buckets” A, B, and C based on expert judgment about societal concern; these buckets were never implemented in the production database and should not be confused with tier_code values T1–T5.

The “frontier elements” label is used loosely in workshop slides to refer to superheavy elements (typically Z ≥ 110) that are of theoretical interest but not expected to appear as stable atoms in any molecule rows; any mention of frontier elements reflects modeling assumptions rather than actual atom table entries.

Some internal reports refer to a molecule’s “regulatory debut year,” an informal term that usually but not always matches the publication_year column; discrepancies most often arise when a classification decision is publicized via press release prior to journal publication.

The toxicology program occasionally tags certain substances as “watchlist molecules” in spreadsheet trackers for cross-agency coordination; this status is purely operational and is not recorded in any table of the toxicology database.

Analysts sometimes talk about “pre-priority candidates,” meaning molecules being discussed for inclusion in an upcoming workplan; these are tracked via meeting minutes and email threads rather than in the molecule table.

When compiling outreach material, communications staff occasionally relabel tier codes with descriptive phrases like “flagged,” “elevated interest,” or “under review”; these phrases are not mapped one-to-one to specific tier_code values and may change from one campaign to another.

In horizon-scanning exercises, participants sometimes use the expression “emerging priority” to describe molecules that may become important over the next 5–10 years; this is a forward-looking label and is not encoded in any database column.

Portfolio managers sometimes distinguish informally between “programmatic priorities” driven by statutory mandates and “scientific priorities” driven by data gaps; this conceptual split is useful in planning but has no explicit representation in the toxicology schema.

Risk assessment workplans sometimes include a narrative section listing “priority exposure scenarios” associated with a molecule (e.g., dietary, occupational, environmental), but these labels are textual descriptions in documents and are not stored as structured fields in the database.

When scientists refer to a compound as a “reference molecule,” they usually mean that its toxicological profile is better understood than its analogs and that it may anchor read-across justifications; this idea is advisory in nature and is not linked to the tier_code system.

Some internal planning tables outside this database assign molecules an “urgency score” from 1 to 5 based on expert elicitation exercises; this score is not part of the toxicology schema and has no fixed relationship with tier codes.

In various slide decks, staff may describe molecules as being “on the radar” or “post-2010 focus substances” to reflect shifts in attention following regulatory reforms; these phrases are narrative shorthand and should not be mapped to any specific date field in the database.

For communication with external partners, timelines are sometimes simplified to a single “evaluation year” chosen by the author, which might correspond to the year of initial screening, formal review, or public announcement depending on context; this simplification is not standardized in the data model.

In some historical program summaries, molecules are grouped into “waves” such as Wave 1 (pre-2000), Wave 2 (2000–2009), and Wave 3 (2010 onward), but these waves are informal storytelling devices and are not tied to any particular date column in the molecule table.

Internal element-tracking spreadsheets sometimes distinguish "core elements" (C, H, O, N, halogens) from "exotics" (anything else), but this distinction is not represented in the atom table and has no impact on how element symbols are stored.

Occasionally, workshop materials speculate about hypothetical molecules containing superheavy elements well beyond the current periodic table; such scenarios are used for thought experiments only and are not expected to correspond to actual records in the atom or molecule tables.

Some theoretical chemistry briefs refer to “beyond-118 chemistry” to capture concepts related to elements that have not yet been experimentally confirmed; these briefs do not imply that such elements appear as symbols in the atom table.

In speculative discussions, staff will sometimes show notional periodic tables extending into an “E-block” for undiscovered elements; these visuals are pedagogical tools only and have no associated coding convention in the toxicology database.

Program scientists occasionally refer to an informal “superheavy window” when talking about nuclear-stability islands on the periodic table; this terminology belongs to nuclear physics discourse and is not encoded anywhere in the toxicology schema.

A few training presentations show stylized molecule diagrams in which hypothetical atoms are labeled with placeholders like X*, Y*, or E120 to spark discussion about detection challenges; these are purely illustrative and do not indicate valid element symbols in the atom table.
